Skip to contents
DGQGSI 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles

DGQGSI

Overview

DGQGSI provides tools for multi-trait selection in plant breeding programs using desired-gain–based selection indices. It integrates two complementary approaches:

  • Desired-Gain Selection Index Optimization (DGSI) Inspired by Joukhadar et al. (2024), this workflow estimates index weights that produce realized genetic gains close to breeder-defined desired gains.

  • Desired-Gain Quadratic Genomic Selection Index (QGSI) Based on Cerón-Rojas et al. (2026), this nonlinear genomic index incorporates both linear and quadratic trait components to capture interactions among traits that a classical linear index would miss.

The key advantage of both methods is that breeders specify desired genetic gains directly — such as “increase yield by 1.5 units while reducing disease score by 1 unit” — rather than economic weights, which are rarely known with precision in practice.

The package also provides:

  • automatic construction of the quadratic desired-gain matrix W_d
  • a high-level pipeline wrapper for running one or both workflows
  • a comparison helper that merges DGSI and QGSI outputs by genotype

Scientific Motivation

Modern crop breeding programs must improve multiple traits simultaneously: yield, stress tolerance, disease resistance, and end-use quality. Classical selection indices solve this by assigning economic weights to each trait, but in practice breeders rarely know the true economic value of a unit change in, say, a disease severity score relative to grain yield.

A more intuitive alternative is to specify desired genetic gains directly. DGQGSI formalises this idea by providing tools to:

  • optimise selection indices using breeder-defined desired gains
  • integrate genomic predictions (GEBVs) into the index
  • incorporate nonlinear trait interactions through the quadratic component
  • compare alternative selection strategies side by side

Methodological Background

1. Desired-Gain Selection Index Optimization (DGSI)

The DGSI finds a weight vector 𝐛\mathbf{b} such that the linear index

I=𝐛⊤𝐗I = \mathbf{b}^\top \mathbf{X}

where:

Symbol Description
𝐛\mathbf{b} Vector of optimised index weights
𝐗\mathbf{X} Matrix of candidate trait values
II Selection index score per genotype

produces realized genetic gains 𝐠\mathbf{g} as close as possible to the breeder’s desired gain vector 𝐝\mathbf{d}. The algorithm in run_desired_gain_index_Joukhadar2024():

  1. Samples candidate desired-gain vectors in the neighbourhood of 𝐝\mathbf{d}.
  2. Computes index coefficients 𝐛\mathbf{b} for each candidate using the phenotypic correlation matrix 𝐏\mathbf{P} and genetic covariance matrix 𝐆\mathbf{G}.
  3. Evaluates the realized gains 𝐠\mathbf{g} on the selected subset.
  4. Retains the weights that minimise the deviation between 𝐠\mathbf{g} and 𝐝\mathbf{d}.

Because the search is stochastic, n_rep independent replicates are run and the best solution is selected by the lowest objective value best_q. The mean_replicate_cor diagnostic summarises index-vector agreement across replicates; values above 0.9 indicate a stable solution.

2. Desired-Gain Quadratic Genomic Selection Index (QGSI)

For genotype ii with GEBV vector 𝛄i\boldsymbol{\gamma}_i, the quadratic index is:

QGSI_DGi=𝐝⊤𝛄i+𝛄i⊤𝐖d𝛄i\text{QGSI\_DG}_i = \mathbf{d}^\top \boldsymbol{\gamma}_i + \boldsymbol{\gamma}_i^\top \mathbf{W}_d \, \boldsymbol{\gamma}_i

where:

Symbol Description
𝐝\mathbf{d} Desired gain vector defined by the breeder
𝛄i\boldsymbol{\gamma}_i Vector of trait GEBVs for genotype ii
𝐖d\mathbf{W}_d Quadratic interaction matrix constructed from 𝐝\mathbf{d}

The linear term 𝐝⊤𝛄i\mathbf{d}^\top \boldsymbol{\gamma}_i rewards GEBVs aligned with the desired direction; the quadratic term 𝛄i⊤𝐖d𝛄i\boldsymbol{\gamma}_i^\top \mathbf{W}_d \, \boldsymbol{\gamma}_i additionally rewards genotypes that combine favourable trait values simultaneously, capturing synergies and penalising trade-offs invisible to a linear index. 𝐖d\mathbf{W}_d is constructed automatically by construct_Wd_from_dg().

Pipeline overview 

Phenotypes / GEBVs
        │
        ▼
Desired Gain Vector (d)
        │
        ├───────────────┐
        │               │
        ▼               ▼
DGSI Optimization     QGSI Computation
        │               │
        ▼               ▼
Selection Index       QGSI Score
        │               │
        └───────┬───────┘
                ▼
       Compare Genotype Rankings

Main Functions

Core workflows

Function Description
run_desired_gain_index_Joukhadar2024() DGSI optimization and genotype ranking
run_qgsi_desired_gain() QGSI scoring from trait GEBVs
run_dgsi_qgsi_pipeline() High-level wrapper for one or both workflows

Supporting tools

Function Description
construct_Wd_from_dg() Automatic construction of the quadratic matrix W_d
compare_dg_and_qgsi() Merge and compare DGSI and QGSI outputs by genotype

Installation

Build Vignettes

# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("FAkohoue/DGQGSI", build_vignettes = TRUE,
  dependencies = TRUE
)

Without Vignettes

# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("FAkohoue/DGQGSI", build_vignettes = FALSE,
  dependencies = TRUE
)

Documentation

Full documentation and tutorials are available at: https://FAkohoue.github.io/DGQGSI/

Quick Start 

library(DGQGSI)
library(data.table)

traits <- c("YLD", "MY", "MI", "BL", "NBL", "VHB")

# Desired gains: positive values for all traits regardless of direction.
# lower_is_better handles sign flipping internally.
dg <- c(YLD = 1.5, MY = 0.5, MI = 0.5, BL = 1, NBL = 1, VHB = 1)

ext   <- system.file("extdata", package = "DGQGSI")
pheno <- fread(file.path(ext, "example_pheno.csv"))
gebv  <- fread(file.path(ext, "example_gebv.csv"))

res <- run_dgsi_qgsi_pipeline(
  mode             = "both",
  init_data        = pheno[, .(GenoID, Family)],
  cand_data        = pheno[, c("GenoID", traits), with = FALSE],
  ref_data         = pheno[, c("GenoID", traits), with = FALSE],
  gebv_data        = gebv[, c("GenoID", traits), with = FALSE],
  trait_cols       = traits,
  dg               = dg,
  lower_is_better  = c("BL", "NBL", "VHB"),
  trait_min_sd     = c(YLD = 0.2, MY = 0.1, MI = 0.1,
                       BL  = 0.1, NBL = 0.1, VHB = 0.1),
  dg_scale_traits    = FALSE,
  qgsi_center_traits = FALSE,
  qgsi_scale_traits  = FALSE,
  merge_outputs    = TRUE,
  debug            = FALSE
)

# Side-by-side DGSI and QGSI rankings
head(res$comparison_result$comparison_table)

# Rank agreement between the two methods
res$comparison_result$correlation_summary

The pipeline returns a named list:

Element Contents
dg_result Full DGSI output including ranked genotypes, realized gains, and diagnostics
W_d_result Automatically constructed quadratic matrix W_d
qgsi_result Full QGSI output including linear/quadratic components per genotype
comparison_result Merged comparison table and correlation summary (when mode = "both")

Documentation

Full documentation, function reference, and tutorials are available at:

https://fakohoue.github.io/DGQGSI

To read the vignette after installation:

vignette("introduction-to-dgqgsi", package = "DGQGSI")

Citation

If you use DGQGSI in published research, please cite:

Akohoue, F. (2026).
DGQGSI: Desired-Gain Selection Index and Quadratic Genomic Selection Index
tools for breeding programs. R package version 0.1.0.
https://github.com/FAkohoue/DGQGSI

References

Joukhadar R, Li Y, Thistlethwaite R, Forrest KL, Tibbits JF, Trethowan R, Hayden MJ (2024). Optimising desired gain indices to maximise selection response. Frontiers in Plant Science 15:1337388. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpls.2024.1337388

Cerón-Rojas JJ, Montesinos-López OA, Montesinos-López A, et al. (2026). Nonlinear genomic selection index accelerates multi-trait crop improvement. Nature Communications 17:1991. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-69890-3
